coredata.dc:title: Utilizing molecular states of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to efficiently harvest outdoor and indoor energy via luminescent solar concentrator
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Mumtaz
Maiyalagan, T.
Lee, Kang Hoon
Choi, In
Ko, Min Jae
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) offer a huge potential for electricity generation due to their ability to harvest direct and diffused light. Among numerous fluorophores for LSCs, carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) are promising candidates due to their non-toxic nature, tunable optical features, and cost-effective synthesis. State-of-the-art LSCs utilizing C-QDs focus on solar energy harvesting, while their potential to harness indoor light for electricity generation is yet to be explored. In this study, we rationally fabricated C-QDs based LSC for efficient energy harvesting under both outdoor and indoor illuminations. Through a facile solvothermal pyrolysis technique, we synthesized the molecular states assisted C-QDs exhibiting a strong absorbance in the visible region that matched well with the outdoor and indoor light spectra. Laminated LSCs were fabricated by coating C-QDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film (photoluminescence quantum yield 71 %) on two glass substrates and joining them with an interlayer of refractive index matching polymer. Given geometry not only protects C-QDs/PVA film from external damage but also prevents light scattering losses that were prominent in an open C-QDs/PVA layer. External efficiency (ηext) of the small-to-large area LSCs under different illuminations were estimated using an analytical approach. The results showed that under outdoor (air mass 1.5 global spectrum) illumination and without scattering background, a large-area (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3) LSC at an optimized concentration of C-QDs exhibited ηext of 3.1 %. When connected with the silicon PV cell, the same LSC yielded a power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) of 0.34 %. Among various indoor illuminations (light-emitting diode (LED)-daylight, LED-warm white, and fluorescent-daylight (CFL)), the best performance was shown under LED-daylight with the ηext and ηPCE of 7.4 % and 0.30 %, respectively. This study offers enormous potential for the adoption of C-QDs based LSC not only in building exteriors (such as windows and facades) but also in the places where artificial lights are used.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy harvesting
Outdoor and indoor light
Luminescent solar concentrator
Carbon quantum dots
Molecular states

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021R1A2C2094794, RS-2024-00408729, and NRF-2022M3J1A1085275). This work was also supported by Korea Environment Industry & Technology Institute (KEITI) through Development of advanced technology for underground infrastructure maintenance in response to urban flooding Project, funded by Korea Ministry of Environment (MOE) (RS-2024-00335526).
This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021R1A2C2094794 , RS-2024-00408729 , and NRF-2022M3J1A1085275 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of C-QDs based LSC fabricated in this study. (c) Photograph of C-QDs based LSC in visible light spectrum.
C-QDs characterizations (a) High-resolution TEM image (b) and size distribution of synthesized C-QDs. (c) XRD spectra of C-QDs. (d) XPS spectrum.

(a) Normalized absorbance and emission spectra of C-QDs in PVA film inset showing the molecular structure of CQDs with carbon core and molecular states. (b) Emission specra at different excitation wavelengths (c) Emission spectra at varied concentrations of C-QDs in PVA film. (d) Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of C-QDs in PVA film representing a decay lifetime τ = 7.3 ns.
(a–d) Spectral overlap between the absorbance of C-QDs and various light sources, (e) Absorption efficiency of LSCs with different C-QDs concentrations.
(a–c) The calculated external efficiency of LSC under different light sources at PLQY (a) 73 %, (b) 80 %, (c) 100 %. (d) Peak emission wavelengths of C-QDs based LSC vs. illumination distance.
J-V curves of C-QDs based LSC under different light sources (a) AM 1.5 G, (b) LED-Daylight-3000k, (c) LED-warm white-5000k, (d) CFL-Daylight-3000k.
PV parameters of C-QDs based LSC (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3) under outdoor illumination condition.
PV parameters of C-QDs based LSC (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3) under indoor illumination condition.

head.ce:title: Utilizing molecular states of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to efficiently harvest outdoor and indoor energy via luminescent solar concentrator

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) offer a huge potential for electricity generation due to their ability to harvest direct and diffused light. Among numerous fluorophores for LSCs, carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) are promising candidates due to their non-toxic nature, tunable optical features, and cost-effective synthesis. State-of-the-art LSCs utilizing C-QDs focus on solar energy harvesting, while their potential to harness indoor light for electricity generation is yet to be explored. In this study, we rationally fabricated C-QDs based LSC for efficient energy harvesting under both outdoor and indoor illuminations. Through a facile solvothermal pyrolysis technique, we synthesized the molecular states assisted C-QDs exhibiting a strong absorbance in the visible region that matched well with the outdoor and indoor light spectra. Laminated LSCs were fabricated by coating C-QDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film (photoluminescence quantum yield 71 %) on two glass substrates and joining them with an interlayer of refractive index matching polymer. Given geometry not only protects C-QDs/PVA film from external damage but also prevents light scattering losses that were prominent in an open C-QDs/PVA layer. External efficiency (ηext) of the small-to-large area LSCs under different illuminations were estimated using an analytical approach. The results showed that under outdoor (air mass 1.5 global spectrum) illumination and without scattering background, a large-area (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3) LSC at an optimized concentration of C-QDs exhibited ηext of 3.1 %. When connected with the silicon PV cell, the same LSC yielded a power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) of 0.34 %. Among various indoor illuminations (light-emitting diode (LED)-daylight, LED-warm white, and fluorescent-daylight (CFL)), the best performance was shown under LED-daylight with the ηext and ηPCE of 7.4 % and 0.30 %, respectively. This study offers enormous potential for the adoption of C-QDs based LSC not only in building exteriors (such as windows and facades) but also in the places where artificial lights are used.

Semitransparent LSC fabricated using visible light active CQDs, which are equally suitable for indoor applications and outdoor applications.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For decades, immense research efforts have been dedicated to developing photovoltaic (PV) technologies for energy conversion under solar irradiance. The recent development of low-powered indoor electronic devices for the Internet of Things (IoT) and wireless sensors has encouraged the scientific community to create photovoltaic technologies that can effectively power these devices by harvesting indoor lights and lowering battery usage [1]. The efforts are realistic since most indoor IoT devices need electrical power in the range of 1–100 microwatts [2].

Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have been regarded as a versatile platform to effectively harvest direct and diffused light [3]. LSCs, even small-area devices, can offer enormous potential for indoor applications because the absorbance of the embedded fluorophore can be fine-tuned to match with the spectra of indoor illuminations. In a simple architecture, an LSC consists of a transparent slab embedded with emissive fluorophores that act as the light-harvesting species [4,5]. With a selective absorption range, fluorophores absorb the shorter wavelength photons over a large surface area and re-emit the longer wavelength photons. A portion of re-mitted photons moves in waveguided mode towards the edges of the slab and are converted into electricity using edge-mounted PV cells [6]. Key factors in designing an efficient LSC device are the spectral properties of the fluorophores, the polymer matrix, and the structure of the device. Ideal fluorophores should possess: (1) high photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) [7], (2) large stokes shift to avoid reabsorption losses [8,9], (3) broad light absorption range [10], and (4) high photostability [11,12]. Secondly, a polymer matrix with a high degree of transparency and low scattering coefficient must have compatibility with embedded fluorophores [13]. Lastly, an LSC should be structured to maximize the light trapping within a waveguide [13]. Generally, LSCs with a refractive index (RI) of 1.5 can trap up to 75 % of light. To date, numerous fluorophores, including conventional dyes, aggregation-induced emissive molecules, and semiconducting quantum dots (QDs) have been proposed for LSC application [14,15]. Recently, semiconducting carbon QDs (C-QDs) have attained much attention compared to other fluorophores as they feature several desirable properties, including cost-effectiveness, accessibility through earth-abundant raw materials, and sustainable synthesis processes [16]. In addition, C-QDs have the advantage of low toxicity, biocompatibility, tunable spectral properties, and resistance to photobleaching [16,17]. In 2017, Li et al. first prepared blue emitting N-doped C-QDs for thin-film LSC (Dimension: 2.5 × 1.6 cm2) and reported 4.76 % optical efficiency (ηext) [18]. Green emitting N-doped C-QDs (65 % PLQY) were also employed in large area LSC (Dimension: 15 × 15 cm2) showing 2.2% ηext [19]. Zhao et al. also fabricated a C-QDs based tandem LSC (Dimension: 10 × 10 cm2) exhibiting an ηext of 1.6 % [20]. Further efforts aiming to improve the LSC performance include the development of tandem and laminated structures as well as a combination of C-QDs with other fluorophores [21,22]. Importantly, research on C-QDs based LSCs has always been focused on the performance evaluation under sunlight, whereas their potential under low-intensity indoor illuminations is neglected.

This study demonstrates newly designed molecular states assisted C-QDs based LSC as an efficient outdoor and indoor light-harvesting device. To the best of our knowledge, this study first explores the potential of C-QDs-based LSCs for indoor light harvesting. At first, a facile solvothermal pyrolysis approach was employed to synthesize the molecular states assisted C-QDs. Owing to the presence of molecular states, synthesized C-QDs exhibited a strong absorbance in the visible range that matched well with the outdoor and indoor light spectra. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) passivation was also performed, which radically improved the stability of C-QDs because of multiple intermolecular interactions between the C-QDs and the PEG network. Second, C-QDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) blend was prepared to fabricate uniform thin films. We realized that an excellent dispersion of C-QDs in PVA effectively suppressed the formation of non-emissive aggregates and helped to maintain high PLQY (71 %). Then, we rationally fabricated the C-QDs based LSC by combining two thin-film waveguides with an interlayer of RI-matching polymer (Fig. 1 ). Such structure not only protects C-QDs/PVA film from accelerated degradation but also avoids light scattering losses, which are more likely to happen in LSCs with single C-QDs/PVA film. Under solar illumination, a large-area (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3) LSC, without scattering background, yielded ηext and power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) of 3.1 % and 0.34 %, respectively. In the case of indoor illuminations, the best performance was shown under light-emitting diode (LED)-daylight with the ηext = 7.4 % and ηPCE = 0.30 %. Thus, this study presents a reliable way to harvest outdoor and indoor light energy using C-QDs based LSCs.

Citric acid, rhodamine dye (RhB), thiourea, PEG-400, ethanol, and PVA were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. RI matching photoreactive monomer NOA65 was purchased from Norland Products, USA. All the chemicals were used without further purification. Micro-cellular foamed Polyethylene Terephthalate sheet as a scattering layer was purchased from a local vendor.

Facile hydrothermal carbonization of organic precursors, including RhB, citric acid, PEG-400, and thiourea, was carried out for the synthesis of C-QDs. The 0.5 g of rhodamine dye, 0.1 g citric acid, and 0.1 g of thiourea were dissolved in 100 ml dimethylformamide via water bath sonication and magnetic stirring. To this solution, 2 ml of polyethylene glycol was mixed using magnetic stirring. The homogeneous solution of precursors was baked at 160 ⁰C for 6 h in a Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal chamber. After carbonization, the CQDs were purified using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut off of 3 kDa, which removes the unreacted molecular impurities. Bigger size particles were separated by adding 30 % acetone of total volume of CQDs, followed by filtration (0.2 µm) and centrifugation (9000 rpm for 40 min). Supernant of centrifugation contains the CQDs, which can be collected via oven drying or freeze-drying. Both small unreacted molecular impurities and larger-sized particles were removed to achieve high quality CQDs.

First, 15 wt% PVA solution was dissolved in water: ethanol (60: 40) mixture by vigorous stirring at room temperature for 24 h. The polymer solution was then mixed with different concentrations (0.1wt% – 0.9 wt%) of as-synthesized C-QDs and degassed using an ultrasonication method. For optical measurements, the final mixtures were coated on thin glass slides (thickness 0.8 mm) by doctor blade technique. Using controlled evaporation, ∼30 µm film thickness was maintained. To make a final LSC device, 0.75 wt% of C-QDs were mixed with a polymer solution and coated on two optically clear low iron glasses (10 × 10 × 0.3 cm3). After the films were completely dried, both glasses were assembled such that coated films faced each other and kept a gap of 20 µm using transparent spaces. RI matching NOA65 was injected between the glasses and allowed to cure under UV light for 3 min. The intensity and wavelength of UV light to polymerize the NOA65 in the mixture were 2.8 mW/cm2 and 365 nm, respectively. The approximate thickness of the final LSC was 0.608 cm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEOL JEM 2100 F) was used to measure the size distribution of CQDs. Wide angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku D/MAX-2500) having the Cu Kα radiation was used to study the graphitic planes of CQDs. Absorbance and emission spectra of C-QDs were measured using UV–Visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (JASCO, FP-8600) respectively. The PLQY was calculated by comparing the integrated area of PL emission signal with a known reference fluorophore, i.e. rhodamine 6 G in ethanol (PLQY=95 %) and quinine sulfate in 0.05 M H2SO4 (PLQY=54 %) [23]. It is important to note that the optical density of both C-QDs and reference solution were kept same for the QY measurements.

For PV measurements, crystalline silicon (c-Si) PV cells purchased from a local vendor were employed. Optically transparent refractive index matching epoxy adhesive (United Adhesives, OE 1582) was used to bind the PV cell with the one edge of the LSC. The other three edges were masked with black tape. To block any direct illuminations, a black mask with a well-defined area was also placed right above the PV-mounted edge of LSC. Four illumination sources were employed in assessing the PV performance of the C-QDs based LSC (1) an air mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5 G) solar simulator (McScience, Republic of Korea) with a power density of 100 mW/cm2, (2) LED-daylight-3000k (McScience, Republic of Korea) with a power density of 7.44 mW/cm2 at 2000 lx, (3) LED-warmlight-5000k (McScience, Republic of Korea) with a power density of 7.40 mW/cm2 at 2000 lx, and (4) Fluorescent light namely CFL-daylight-3000k (McScience, Republic of Korea) also with a power density of 5.50 mW/cm2 at 2000 lx.

The C-QDs were synthesized using an organic precursor, i.e., Rhodamine B, which serves as a source for the formation of molecular states. In addition, the PEG-400 serves as a passivating agent to avoid the aggregation of C-QDs, hence yielding a higher concentration of uniformly sized C-QDs. A uniform circular morphology of C-QDs can be observed, as shown in TEM analysis (Fig. 2 (a)). The lattice fringes of C-QDs crystalline core are evident that the carbonization of the precursors has successfully taken place [24,25]. The average size distribution of C-QDs was observed in the range of 2 – 10 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). Despite the broad size distribution, the color of C-QDs was orange only, which could be related to the fact that emission is not dependent on size or quantum confinement effect [26]. The XRD pattern of C-QDs (Fig. 2(c)) showed a broad peak centered around 23.7⁰, with a calculated d-spacing of 0.375 nm. This spacing is consistent with the (002) plan of graphite (0.33 nm), whereas a minor increase in spacing is observed due to the high surface functionality of C-QDs. The mutual repulsions between highly electronegative surface functional groups of C-QDs result in slightly higher lattice spacing [27–29]. It is important to note that the presence of the crystalline plane confirms that the RhB has successfully carbonized to yield the C-QDs.

The details regarding atomic composition and chemical bonding were probed using XPS, as shown in Fig. 2(d). As expected, the XPS survey of C-QDs revealed the peaks for S 2 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s at characteristic binding energies, present with an atomic ratio of 0.02:1:0.06:0.76, respectively. The content of N and S is relatively low; however, their role in tuning the emission is significant (discussed in the next section). The deconvoluted peaks of these elements are given in Fig. S1. A significant portion of surface groups with carbon backbone can be observed, as shown in the high-resolution spectrum [Fig. S1(a)]. Mainly, C1s is forming CC/C = C/CH bonds (284.5 eV), C = N, C = O bonds (285.6 eV), and CO, CO-C, CN bonds (287.8 eV) [30–32]. Similarly, nitrogen exists in graphitic (401.6 eV), pyrrolic (400.5 eV), and pyridinic (399.5 eV) states, which confirms that carbonization of RhB and thiourea has resulted in C-QDs with other states of nitrogen [30–32]. The S 1 s showed S-C, S-H, and S-S bonds centered at a binding energy of 163.3, 164.7, and 167.9 eV, respectively. The presence of graphitic nitrogen and S-C bonds shows that the nitrogen and sulfur both are doped in the carbon lattice, not only on the surface [30–32].

The optical properties of C-QDs, including absorbance and emission, are critically important in determining the LSC performance. In most cases, optical properties were obtained by loading C-QDs into polymer matrixes such as polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), or polylauryl methacrylate (PLMA). Such polymers often show less compatibility with C-QDs, which leads to the degradation of the C-QDs’ passivation layer, triggering a drop in the PLQY. Another harmful effect is the formation of non-emissive aggregates, especially when C-QDs are employed in higher concentrations. These aggregates not only cause PL quenching but also lead to severe scattering losses within the LSCs. Herein, PVA was selected as a preferred matrix, allowing better dispersion of C-QDs and higher PLQY due to enhanced surface passivation effect. We successfully attained the uniform film through the controlled evaporation of the solvent mixture. The UV–visible absorbance spectrum of C-QDs/PVA film showed the first peak around 300 – 360 nm, which is related to n–π* transitions (Fig. 3 (a)). The second significantly prominent absorption peak in the 440 – 550 nm wavelength range is related to the surface states and molecular states [33,34]. Generally, the n–π* transitions are related to excitations occurring in carbon domains of the C-QDs core, which are possible at high energies [33]. In contrast, the surface states and molecular states are formed because of non-metallic dopant and conjugated molecular moiety attached to the C-QDs core, respectively [34]. These surface states and molecular states produce intermediate states between HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbitals) – LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals). The newly formed energy levels reduce the HOMO – LUMO energy level; hence a red-shifted emission in visible spectrums can be realized. For instance, here, the emission of C-QDs was centered around 550 – 650 nm. The PLQY of synthesized C-QDs in PVA film was recorded to be 71 %. Higher PLQY is achieved due to the better dispersion of C-QDs in PVA. Mechanistically the improved PLQY may be explained based on compression of the surface passivation layer in C-QDs embedded in a compact PVA environment. Besides, rich hydrogen bonds can make molecular states on the surfaces of C-QDs rigidified, resulting in a superior PLQY. Further, the density of electrons near the Fermi level in the N, S co-doped C-QDs is higher, which increases the probability of radiative recombination, as compared to the undoped system. Another aspect evident in Fig. 3(a) is the overlap of absorption and emission spectra, which is responsible for excessive energy losses. This overlap and similar quantum yield is also observed in RhB dye, therefore congenitally similar optical properties were observed for RhB-derived CQDs.

The emission of C-QDs was further studied in detail under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3(b)) and various concentrations (Fig. 3(c)). Fig. 3b clearly illustrates the excitation-independent emission characteristics of C-QDs in PVA that can be ascribed to molecular states bound on the central carbon core. Molecular states are formed by the RhB molecular moieties, which are decorated on the carbon core, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a). High energy absorption and emission are mainly due to the carbon core whereas low energy transitions of CCQDs originate from the organic fluorophores on the surface [35]. During the hydrothermal carbonization, the RhB molecules are not completely carbonized to form C-QDs core. The conjugated organic molecule of RhB moieties attached to the C-QDs surface causes constructive FRET with carbon core, thus showing emission similar to RhB molecules [35]. Conversely, the excitation-dependent emission could be possible due to surface states as well as oxidized-carbon-domains from the C-QD core as reported in previous studies [36,37]. We also observed the effect of C-QDs concentration on the emission intensity. A linear improvement in the emission intensity was observed till the concentration was increased to 0.75 wt%, as shown in (Fig. 3(c)). Further increase in the concentration (0.9 wt%) led to emissions quenching, which may be due to the formation of non-emissive aggregates at high concentrations and self-absorption losses. Destructive FRET-induced quenching of optical properties at higher concentrations is a well-understood phenomenon in C-QDs [24,25,38]. Aggregates were not observed via microscopy and colloidal instability; however, optical properties are quenched due to parasitic self-reabsorption and spectral overlap [Fig. 3(a)]. Specifically, the spectral overlap is increased at higher concentrations, which can be related to detrimental optical properties at higher concentrations.The charge recombination dynamics were further probed using time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL), as shown in (Fig. 3(d)). The fitted curve's the TRPL curve was monoexponential, showing that the recombinations are dominantly radiative. The τaverage was 7.3 ns, which indicates the fast charge recombinations in the C-QDs. Similarly, the TRPL curve for C-QDs colloid also showed a mono-exponential decay, which could be related to dominantly radiative recombination (Fig. S3). Compared to solid-state TRPL, the average decay time of C-QDs colloids is much higher, i.e. τAVG of 12.4 ns was observed. Comparatively, a longer dwell time is due to the solvation states formed by solvent interactions and free surface vibrations in colloidal form.

One of the key objectives of this study is to investigate the performance of C-QDs based LSCs under various illumination conditions, especially indoor lights. Four different illumination sources, i.e., AM 1.5 G spectrum (sunlight), LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k, have been employed. The examined illumination sources show distinct spectral irradiances that cover different wavelength regions. Fig. 4 (a–d) shows normalized C-QDs absorbance and the spectral irradiances of the different illumination sources. It is indicated that both outdoor and indoor light can be effectively harvested using C-QDs based LSCs since C-QDs absorbance spectrum significantly overlaps with the incident irradiance spectra. To quantify the capacity of C-QDs to harvest the incident illuminations available for energy generation, the absorption efficiency of LSCs at different concentrations of C-QDs was calculated (Eq. S1, Supporting information). As can be seen in Fig. 4(e), ηabs under LED-daylight-3000k remained highest among all the light sources, e.g. ηabs of 0.1 wt% C-QDs based LSC was 5.5 %, 12.5 %, 8.3 %, and 4.3 % under AM 1.5 G, LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k, respectively. Moreover, ηabs increased linearly with the increasing concentration of C-QDs in all the cases, e.g., ηabs was 14.5 % (AM 1.5 G), 12.5 % (LED-daylight-3000k), 8.3 % (LED-warmlight-5000k), and 4.3 % (CFL-daylight-3000k) at 0.9 wt% C-QDs.

A widely reported theoretical approach was employed to obtain the ηext of LSCs having different lengths (Eq. S2, Supporting information) [39,40]. Where, ηext accounts for both light harvesting ability and waveguiding losses and is defined as the ratio of the number of photons emitting out of LSC edges to the total number of incident photons [41]. LSC employing 0.75 wt% C-QDs was selected for further measurements since it showed the highest emissions (Fig. 3c). In Fig. 5 a, we observed the effect of increasing the length of LSC on its optical performance under different illuminations. Using the PLQY equal to the experimental value (71 %), the ηext always remained higher under LED-daylight-3000k when compared to the other illumination sources. For example, 2.5 cm LSC exhibited the ηext of 4.8 %, 11.6 %, 7.1 %, and 4 % under AM 1.5 G, LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k, respectively. At a length equal to 100 cm, the obtained ηext was 0.7 %, 1.6 %, 1 %, and 0.5 % under AM 1.5 G, LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k, respectively. In agreement with the existing studies, a decrease in ηext with the increasing length of LSC is predictable due to the presence of various losses such as reabsorption, surface scattering, and escape cone losses during the propagation of the photons from the LSC center to its edges [42]. In Fig. 5b and c, we examined the ηext at higher PLQYs, i.e., 85 % and 100 %, while keeping other experimental parameters (square geometry, absorption, and emission spectra) the same. As expected, ηext increased significantly at higher PLQYs. For example, in the case of 10 cm LSC under AM 1.5 G, improving the PLQY from 71 % to 100 % leads to an increase in ηext from 3.1 % to 7.2 %. Similarly, for 10 cm LSC under LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k, an increase of PLQY to 100 % yields ηext of 15.4 %, 10.8 % and 6.3 %, respectively. This assessment is important because an increment in PLQY is possible using the plasmonic effect [4,43].

As elaborated above, various loss channels lead to a decrease in ηext when LSC length increases. To validate these losses at different lengths of LSCs, we obtained the peak emission wavelengths as a function of illumination distance from the light collecting edge of the LSC (Fig. 5d). For this purpose, a long test bed C-QDs based LSC (15 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.3 cm) was prepared. The peak emission wavelength was documented at different distances between the detector edge and the position of the excitation source. An insert of Fig. 5d shows a schematic of a setup for distance-dependent edge emission measurement. The peak emission wavelength of C-QDs based LSC was 576 nm and 595 nm at the excitation distance of 2.5 cm and 15 cm, respectively. At increased length, the probability of photon reabsorption and consequent red-shifted re-emission increased significantly.

Real-world implementation of LSC requires its integration with PV cells to form a unified LSC-PV device. The key figure-of-merit to quantify the electrical performance of LSC is ηPCE, which can be defined as the ratio of electrical power produced by LSC-PV devices to the total power incident on the top surface LSC. To obtain the ηPCE of large-area C-QDs based LSC (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3), one edge (10 × 0.6 cm3) of the LSC was coupled with PV cell, while other edges were masked with black tape. As suggested by Yang et al., the overall performance of LSC was then corrected by multiplying short-circuit current (ISC) by 4 [4,5]. We also placed a black mask with a well-defined area right above the PV-mounted edge of LSC. This was done to block any direct illuminations, which would otherwise lead to the overestimation of ILSC. Measurements were performed under two configurations, (1) without scattering background and (2) with scattering background, and ηPCE was calculated using Eq. S4, Supporting Information (Refer to Experimental section for detailed illumination conditions).

First, the electrical performance of C-QDs based LSC was obtained under outdoor conditions, i.e., AM 1.5 G spectrum. As can be seen in Fig. 6 a and Table 1 , our device (without scattering background) exhibited JSC = 0.98 mA/cm2 ± 0.05, open circuit voltage (VOC) = 0.51 V ± 0.004, fill factor (FF) = 66.5 % ± 2, and ηPCE = 0.34 % ± 0.006. While, with scattering background, JSC, VOC, FF, and ηPCE were 2.15 mA/cm2 ± 0.04, 0.55 V ± 0.004, 69 % ± 4, and 0.81 % ± 0.004 respectively. To evaluate the electrical performance of C-QDs based LSC-PV device under indoor lighting conditions, J-V curves were obtained by illuminating the device with three light sources i.e., LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k (Fig. 6b–d). Table 2 summarizes the PV parameters of C-QDs based LSC under all indoor illuminations. As evident, C-QDs based LSC (without scattering background) exhibited ηPCE of 0.30 % ± 0.005 with the corresponding JSC, VOC, and FF values of 15.6 µA/cm2 ± 0.7, 0.29 V ± 0.001, and 50 % ± 2, respectively under LED-daylight-3000k. Using other indoor illumination sources such as LED-warmlight-5000k and CFL-daylight-3000k leads to a further reduction in JSC, VOC, FF, and ηPCE, as can be seen in Table 2. Interestingly, all PV parameters obtained under indoor illuminations were lower than those under AM 1.5 G spectrum. The electrical performance of C-QDs based LSC under several lighting conditions can be explained based on variations in the intensities of incident lights. As stated in the Experimental section, the intensities of indoor illuminations were more than two orders of magnitude lower than that of AM 1.5 G light, JSC decreased accordingly from ∼0.98 mA/cm2 to ∼10–16 µA/cm2 without scattering background. Even when scattering background was applied, JSC was 30 µA/cm2 ± 0.4, 27 µA/cm2 ± 0.5, and 20 µA/cm2 ± 1 under LED-daylight-3000k, LED-warmlight-5000k, and CFL-daylight-3000k, respectively. Moreover, the reduction in VOC and FF is also evident from Fig. 6, which is certainly due to their logarithmic dependence on JSC. The performance of LSC is strongly associated with the PLQY of the luminophore, as the intensity of edge emission is directly related to the intensity of radiative emissions. In addition, the wavelength of emission is another important parameter determining the Rayleigh scattering and associated pathlengths. For instance, higher penetration depths and longer photon transport paths are possible for long wavelength emissions [44]. Therefore, our device showed a comparable ηPCE despite low QY and spectral overlap.

In order to test the stability of LSCs, the performance difference after constant exposure to a UV lamp for 72 h. C-QDs-LSC retained 82 % of its initial performance, compared to a rapid loss of performance of dye-based LSC to 59 % of initial performance [45]. Relative to dye, the photostability of C-QDs is significantly high because of their inorganic nature. On the contrary, the excited triplet states of common organic fluorophores sensitize the ground states of oxygen molecules in the vicinity, which produces reactive oxygen species (ROS) [46]. The highly reactive nature of ROS causes cleavage of organic dye molecules, forming nonradiative intermediates. Although the probability of the formation of triplet states is low (∼10–3–10–4 or less) in rhodamines, the degradation profile is matched with strongly triplet states forming dyes [46]. This phenomenon may be related to the reactive radical intermediates formed due to photoionization in the first or higher excited electronic states under intense UV light [46].

Compared to previous reports on C-QDs [Table S1], our focus is studying the longer wavelength emission C-QDs for indoor environments. Most of the previous reports focused on the fabrication of LSC for outdoor conditions, by employing C-QDs having blue or green emission [47–51]. Unlike other works, we primarily explored the potential of C-QDs with molecular-states showing orange emission, focusing on effective harvesting of indoor light conditions. Furthermore, the production yield of C-QD synthesized using our method gives a high production yield of above 80 %, using a straightforward synthesis method.

The ηPCE achieved by the C-QDs based LSC under different types of incident lights provides high prospects for its implementation in various fields, including outdoor applications (e.g., energy harvesting windows and BIPVs) to indoor applications (e.g., IoT and portable electronics, etc.). The higher ηPCE under various illuminations also addresses the artistic potential of C-QDs based LSCs. For example, C-QDs films can be applied on transparent waveguides for creating artistic images [52]. This system, when combined with light-emitting diodes (LEDs), will possibly benefit from the power-producing features of the LSC. The energy harvested under various illuminations can be used to activate the LEDs, triggering the fluorescent image to glow with higher intensity. Such applications can make the use of smaller C-QDs based LSCs more effective than their large-area counterparts.

In summary, we presented a rationally designed laminated LSC based on molecular states-assisted C-QDs as emissive fluorophores and PVA as a matrix. Molecular states assisted C-QDs, prepared by facile solvothermal pyrolysis approach, showed a wide absorbance with a peak centered at 507 nm and excitation-independent emission in the 500–700 nm wavelength range with a peak maximum at 575 nm. Well-defined absorption and emission spectra have been attributed to surface-bound molecular states. Surprisingly, when dispersed in PVA to fabricate laminated LSC devices, C-QDs exhibited superior PLQY (71 %). Such high PLQY may be ascribed to the enhancement of the passivation effect upon dispersing the C-QDs into a more restrictive PVA environment. In a view of practical applications, the optical and electrical performance of C-QDs based LSC was evaluated under outdoor (AM 1.5 G spectrum) and indoor illuminations (LED-daylight, LED-warmlight, and CFL-daylight). At an optimized concentration of C-QDs in PVA, the LSC device (10 × 10 × 0.6 cm3) was found to exhibit a maximum ηext and ηint of 3.1 and 26 %, under AM 1.5 G spectrum, respectively. Not only this, the same LSC yielded the ηext as high as 7.4 % under LED-daylight, the highest value compared to that obtained under other illumination sources. Further, J-V measurements revealed that the highest ηPCE of 0.34 % was obtained under AM 1.5 G spectrum (without scattering background). On the other hand, among indoor light conditions, the LSC-PV device outperformed under LED daylight with the ηPCE of 0.30 %. The results suggest that C-QDs based LSCs can effectively serve as aesthetically appealing “wireless power suppliers” for the uninterrupted functioning of various indoor electronic gadgets such as IoT devices, decorative elements, and network sensors.

Mumtaz Ali: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. T. Maiyalagan: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Kang Hoon Lee: Visualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. In Choi: Data curation, Validation, Funding acquisition. Min Jae Ko: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021R1A2C2094794, RS-2024-00408729, and NRF-2022M3J1A1085275). This work was also supported by Korea Environment Industry & Technology Institute (KEITI) through Development of advanced technology for underground infrastructure maintenance in response to urban flooding Project, funded by Korea Ministry of Environment (MOE) (RS-2024-00335526).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104953.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Mateen, M. Ali, S.Y. Lee, S.H. Jeong, M.J. Ko, and S.K. Hong, “Tandem structured luminescent solar concentrator based on inorganic carbon quantum dots and organic dyes,” Sol. Energy, vol. 190, no. April, pp. 488–494, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.solener.2019.08.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photobleaching of organic fluorophores: quantitative characterization, mechanisms, protection*
Methods Appl. Fluoresc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.P. Demchenko, “Photobleaching of organic fluorophores: quantitative characterization, mechanisms, protection*,” Methods Appl. Fluoresc., vol. 8, no. 2, p. 22001, 2020, doi: 10.1088/2050-6120/ab7365.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vacuum-boosting precise synthetic control of highly bright solid-state carbon quantum dots enables efficient light emitting diodes
Small

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, G. Liu, M. Wang, Y. Song, Q. Jing, and H. Zhao, “Vacuum-boosting precise synthetic control of highly bright solid-state carbon quantum dots enables efficient light emitting diodes,” Small, vol. n/a, no. n/a, p. 2401812, May 2024, doi: 10.1002/smll.202401812.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controllable and large-scale synthesis of carbon quantum dots for efficient solid-state optical devices
Nano Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li et al., “Controllable and large-scale synthesis of carbon quantum dots for efficient solid-state optical devices,” Nano Energy, vol. 122, p. 109289, 2024, doi: 10.1016/j.nanoen.2024.109289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient and high color rendering index multilayer luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal carbon quantum dots
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lin, L. Wang, Q. Jing, and H. Zhao, “Highly efficient and high color rendering index multilayer luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal carbon quantum dots,” Chem. Eng. J., vol. 481, p. 148441, 2024, doi: 10.1016/j.cej.2023.148441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-transparent luminescent solar concentrators based on carbon nanodots in the siloxane matrix with ultrahigh quantum yields and optical transparency at high-loading contents
J. Phys. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Talite et al., “Visible-transparent luminescent solar concentrators based on carbon nanodots in the siloxane matrix with ultrahigh quantum yields and optical transparency at high-loading contents,” J. Phys. Chem. Lett., vol. 11, no. 2, pp. 567–573, Jan. 2020, doi: 10.1021/acs.jpclett.9b03539.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient carbon dots with reversibly switchable green–red emissions for trichromatic white light-emitting diodes
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yuan et al., “Highly efficient carbon dots with reversibly switchable green–red emissions for trichromatic white light-emitting diodes,” ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, vol. 10, no. 18, pp. 16005–16014, May 2018, doi: 10.1021/acsami.8b02379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Printed luminescent solar concentrators: artistic renewable energy
Energy Build.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. ter Schiphorst et al., “Printed luminescent solar concentrators: artistic renewable energy,” Energy Build., vol. 207, p. 109625, 2020, doi: 10.1016/j.enbuild.2019.109625.
